Motley County

Discover the Quanah Parker Trail arrows and rich history in Motley County.

Table of Contents

  • Introduction to Motley County

    • Arrow #1: Matador

  • Visitor Information & Resources

Introduction to Motley County

Motley County’s history is defined by its role as a geographic thoroughfare where the paths of Comanches, conquistadors, and cattlemen converged. Before European settlement, the region was a critical artery of the Comanchería, with the Valley of Tears near the Quitaque Peaks serving as a notorious trade rendezvous where Comancheros exchanged goods with the Kiowa and Comanche for livestock and captives. Later, the U.S. Army solidified these informal indigenous routes into the Mackenzie Trail, a military supply road carved by Colonel Ranald S. Mackenzie during the Red River War to connect frontier forts to the high plains battlegrounds. This military infrastructure eventually paved the way for the great cattle drives of the late 19th century; legendary operations like the Matador Ranch utilized these same corridors to move massive herds to railheads, effectively transforming the county from a nomadic wintering ground into a permanent ranching empire. \n\n for paragraphs)

Arrows in Motley County

Arrow #1: Matador

More Details for Arrow #1

Location: Corner of Main Street and Stewart Avenue in Matador

Coordinates: 34.014705, -100.821976

Installed: August 10, 2011

Dedicated: October 15, 2011

Marker Text:

Quanah Parker spoke downtown, 1909 Matador Ranch hosted chief & wives other Comanches camped N of jail

Get Directions via Google Maps

Arrow #74: Roaring Springs

More Details for Arrow #74

Location: Entrance to Roaring Springs Ranch Club about six-tenths of a mile west of Highway 70 on Farm-to-Market Road 3203. Located on the south side of the road.

Coordinates: 33.8605237, -100.8604477

Installed: June 10, 2014

Dedicated: April 16, 2015

Marker Text:

Comanche guides led JJ Sturm here, a favored campsite, seeking Quanah but finding Chief Blackbeard, 1875

Get Directions via Google Maps

Welcome to the Quanah Parker Trail!

Explore the historical arrow markers commemorating Quanah Parker and the Comanche people across the Texas Plains Trail Region. Use the map on the homepage or navigate via the menu to learn more about this effort.
